title: Checkpoint A — Quiz: Levels 1–4
intro:
## Quick gut-check

17 multiple-choice questions covering Python basics, NumPy, pandas, and scikit-learn. Tap an answer to see if it's right and read a one-line explanation. Your picks are saved locally — score updates at the top.

Missed something? Jump back to the relevant level (link at the end of the explanation).

quiz.question: What does `xs[1:4]` give when `xs = [10, 20, 30, 40, 50]`?
quiz.explain: Slicing is **end-exclusive**: `[1:4]` gives indices 1, 2, 3 → `[20, 30, 40]`. The element at index 4 is not included. (Level 1.2)

quiz.question: Inside `if ___ :` for comparing `x` to `5`, which is correct Python?
quiz.explain: `=` is **assignment**, `==` is **comparison**. Python has no `===` (that's JavaScript). Mixing up `=` and `==` is the #1 beginner bug. (Level 1.4)

quiz.question: Given `for i, v in enumerate(["a", "b", "c"]):`, what is `i` on the second iteration?
quiz.explain: `enumerate` yields `(index, value)` pairs starting at 0. Second iteration → `(1, "b")`, so `i = 1`. (Level 1.3)

quiz.question:
What does this print?

```python
labels = ["spam", "ham", "spam"]
binary = [1 if x == "spam" else 0 for x in labels]
print(binary)
```
quiz.explain: The conditional expression `1 if x == "spam" else 0` evaluates per-element. "spam"→1, "ham"→0, "spam"→1. (Level 1.7)

quiz.question: Which call parses a JSON **string** back into a Python object?
quiz.explain: Mnemonic: `s` = **string**. `loads`/`dumps` work on strings; `load`/`dump` (no s) work on file handles. `dumps` *creates* a JSON string from a Python object; `loads` parses one. (Level 1.10)

quiz.question: What is `a.shape` after `a = np.arange(12).reshape(3, 4)`?
quiz.explain: `reshape(3, 4)` returns a view with 3 rows and 4 columns, so `.shape == (3, 4)`. Total elements (12) must match `3 * 4`. (Level 2.3)

quiz.question: Given 0/1 prediction and label arrays, what does `(predictions == actual).mean()` compute?
options: Raises an error — can't average booleans
quiz.explain: `==` returns a boolean array. NumPy treats `True` as 1 and `False` as 0, so `.mean()` gives the fraction `True` — exactly accuracy. (Level 2.2)

quiz.question: On a 2D array `data`, what does `data[:, -1]` select?
quiz.explain: `[rows, cols]`: `:` means all rows, `-1` means the last column index. So this returns every row's last value as a 1D array. (Level 2.4)

quiz.question: Why call `.copy()` before mutating a NumPy array you got from a slice or `np.where`?
options: It's faster than mutating in place
Slices are views — mutating them changes the original array too
NumPy requires it for any assignment
quiz.explain: Slices and many indexing operations return **views** that share memory with the original. Without `.copy()`, modifying the view silently corrupts the original. Every poisoning / perturbation script must `.copy()` first. (Level 2.5)

quiz.question: If `X.shape == (100, 5)`, what shape does `X.mean(axis=0)` have?
quiz.explain: `axis=0` **collapses rows** (the 0-th dimension) → one mean per column → shape `(5,)`. `axis=1` would collapse columns and give `(100,)`. (Level 2.8)

quiz.question: Cleanest way to select rows where `df.label` is `"exfil"` OR `"c2"`:
quiz.explain: `.isin([...])` is the standard OR-over-many. Bare `or`/`in` don't work on Series — they raise `ValueError: truth value of a Series is ambiguous`. (Level 3.2)

quiz.question: What does `df.label.value_counts(normalize=True)` return?
options: Raw counts of each unique value
quiz.explain: `normalize=True` divides each count by the total, giving proportions. This is how you check class balance before training. (Level 3.5)

quiz.question: Vectorized way to make a new column `total = bytes_in + bytes_out` in pandas:
options: `for i in range(len(df)): df.loc[i, "total"] = df.loc[i, "bytes_in"] + df.loc[i, "bytes_out"]`
quiz.explain: Column arithmetic is element-wise — no loop, no `.apply`. The looping version works but is 100–1000× slower on real datasets. (Level 3.3)

quiz.question: What does `train_test_split(X, y, test_size=0.2)` return?
quiz.explain: Fixed order: **X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test**. Both X's then both y's. Getting this wrong silently feeds your test data into training. Always pin `random_state=` too. (Level 4.1)

quiz.question: Why must `StandardScaler` be fit only on the **training set**, not the full dataset?
options: Test set is usually too small to fit on
It would leak test-set statistics into the model — optimistic, fake-good scores
quiz.explain: Fitting on all data leaks test statistics (mean/std) into preprocessing. Use a `Pipeline` to chain scaler + model — it handles this automatically. (Level 4.3)

quiz.question: Imbalanced security data: 95% benign. A lazy model always predicts "benign" and gets 95% accuracy. Which metric exposes that it's useless?
quiz.explain: Recall = TP / (TP + FN). If the model never predicts "malicious", TP = 0 → recall = 0. Accuracy hides this; that's why security ML reports precision/recall/F1, not accuracy alone. (Level 4.4)

quiz.question: In `Pipeline([("scaler", StandardScaler()), ("clf", LogisticRegression())])`, what does `pipe.fit(X_tr, y_tr)` actually do?
options: Fits scaler and clf independently on the raw X_tr
Fits scaler on X_tr, transforms X_tr, then fits clf on the scaled X_tr
Fits clf only; the scaler is applied at predict time
Fits scaler, then fits clf on raw (un-scaled) X_tr
quiz.explain: A Pipeline runs each transformer's `fit_transform` in order, then `fit` on the final estimator with the transformed data. At predict time, the same fitted transformers apply to new data. (Level 4.3)
